coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic synthesis of high-concentration hydrogen peroxide by efficient photothermal synergy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenjia
Song, Jizong
Ren, Weijie
Wu, Jie
Chang, Qing
Li, Ning
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: We here report an energy-saving and cost-effective strategy to produce high-concentration H2O2 in commercial sponge with aqueous storage functions. Thereinto, the rationally designed hybrid consisting of thiourea-group-grafted covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) and carbon dots (CDs) uniformly wraps the skeletons of the porous sponge to serve as photocatalytic system. This system allows solar radiation to be localized at the surface of photocatalyst and meantime suppresses the tremendous energy consumption of evaporation significantly by a simple seal way, thus giving rise to the required reaction temperature. With it, not only solar-converted heat as by-product in photocatalysis is fully utilized in H2O2 production, but also high-concentration H2O2 solution (105.2 mM for per gram photocatalyst in 1 h) is gained due to catalysis confinement effect. For application, our presented design exhibits more superior activities in degrading different organic pollutants (such as Rhodamine B, phenols and tetracycline) than conventional particulate photocatalysis thanks to in situ formation of concentrated H2O2 in sponge.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photosynthesis
Hydrogen peroxide
Covalent triazine frameworks
Carbon dots
Organic pollutant degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation illustration of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (b) FTIR spectra of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (c-f) XPS C 1s spectra of (c) DC-CTFs, (d) Bpt-CTFs, (e) Bpt@DC-CTFs and (f) CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (g and h) TEM images of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (i) HRTEM image of a typical carbon nanoparticle incorporated into CTFs; (j) The images of Elemental mapping for CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (b) Energy band edge potentials of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (c) H2O2 production rates from DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs irradiated with simulated sunlight; (d) The effects of CDs mass on the H2O2 production rates of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (e) The dependence of H2O2 production rates of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs with the mass ratio of DC to Bpt; (f) Photocurrent responses from DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (g) EIS spectra of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (h) PL spectra of Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs; (i) TFS spectra of Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs.

(a) Schematic illustration for the fabrication processes of Device 1; (b-d) SEM images of photocatalyst-modified sponge; (e) Comparison of H2O2 production rates for different cases; (f) Surface temperature increases with the irradiation time for three cases in Device 1, Control 1 and Control 2.

(a) The effects of solar intensities on H2O2 production rates; (b) The surface temperature changes with irradiation time under different solar intensities; (c) The stability of H2O2 solution (5 mM) at the different temperatures; (d-f) Comparison of the pollutant degradation ratio of RhB (d), TC (e) and phenol (f) between Device 1 and PC after one hour solar irradiation under the same conditions; The insets showing UV–visible spectra of RhB (d), TC (e) and phenol (f) before and after one hour irradiation under photocatalysis of Device 1 and PC.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic synthesis of high-concentration hydrogen peroxide by efficient photothermal synergy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We here report an energy-saving and cost-effective strategy to produce high-concentration H2O2 in commercial sponge with aqueous storage functions. Thereinto, the rationally designed hybrid consisting of thiourea-group-grafted covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) and carbon dots (CDs) uniformly wraps the skeletons of the porous sponge to serve as photocatalytic system. This system allows solar radiation to be localized at the surface of photocatalyst and meantime suppresses the tremendous energy consumption of evaporation significantly by a simple seal way, thus giving rise to the required reaction temperature. With it, not only solar-converted heat as by-product in photocatalysis is fully utilized in H2O2 production, but also high-concentration H2O2 solution (105.2 mM for per gram photocatalyst in 1 h) is gained due to catalysis confinement effect. For application, our presented design exhibits more superior activities in degrading different organic pollutants (such as Rhodamine B, phenols and tetracycline) than conventional particulate photocatalysis thanks to in situ formation of concentrated H2O2 in sponge.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a critical green oxidant that is widely applied in industries and daily lives including disinfection, contaminant remediation, pulping and bleaching [1,2]. However, its production highly relies on the anthraquinone oxidation method, suffering from the burden of energy-consuming and environmental impacts [3,4]. Solar-driven H2O2 synthesis from water and oxygen has received much attention as a green, safe, and sustainable process [5,6]. To pursue high H2O2 yields, various photocatalysts have been developed to efficiently trigger the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and/or the water oxidation reaction (WOR) with solar energy input [7,8]. In consideration of structural designability and selectivity for two-electron redox reactions, conjugated polymer photocatalysts possess more favorable performance for H2O2 generation than inorganic semiconductors [9–11]. Among them, covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) exhibit promising characteristics including molecularly precise backbones, physicochemical stability, synthetic diversity, visible-light responses, which offer a broad molecular-level platform for designing ideal photocatalysts with targeted band structures [12,13]. To suppress rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in polymer photocatalysts, it has been developed to design donor–acceptor structures and creating molecular heterojunctions [14–16]. However, the charge separation and transfer efficiency still falls far behind expectations, resulting in low H2O2 production rate. Currently, one can only achieve very low concentration of H2O2 (around 1 mM h−1) in reaction solution [17,18]. Although further concentration technique could enable the content of H2O2 in solution to increase, it always requires complex infrastructure as well as extra energy consuming, leading to a high production cost [19,20].

Recent works have demonstrated that thermal energy can boost H2O2 production. Kou et al. [21] reported a bipyridine based covalent organic framework that shows a higher solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency at 333 K (1.08 %) than 298 K (0.57 %). Using a porphyrin supramolecular photocatalyst, Zhang et al. [22] acquired a total H2O2 accumulation of 2.51 mM within 4 h at 353 K, which is more thantentimes that at 293 K (0.22 mM). Accordingly, the rational utilization of solar-converted heat in catalytic process could be an efficient photosynthesis strategy to enhance H2O2 yields. However, most of photocatalysts are only responsive in the limited wavelength region and their photothermal conversion efficiency is relatively low, thus unable to raise reaction temperatures [23,24].

In this work, we presented an energy-saving and cost-effective strategy to produce high-concentration H2O2 in commercial sponge with aqueous storage functions. In this strategy, carbon dots (CDs) as hole extractors were incorporated into thiourea-functionalized CTFs by two-step polymerization and then the resultant hybrid product was uniformly wrapped the skeletons of the melamine sponge substrate with the help of in-situ gelation reaction of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to serve as photocatalytic system. This system allowed solar radiation to be localized at the surface of photocatalyst and meantime suppressed the tremendous energy consumption of evaporation significantly by a simple seal way, thus giving rise to the required reaction temperature. With it, not only solar-converted heat as by-product in photocatalysis was fully utilized in H2O2 production, but also high-concentration H2O2 solution was gained due to catalysis confinement effect. For application, our presented design exhibited more superior activities in depredating different organic pollutants than conventional particulate photocatalysis thanks to in situ formation of concentrated H2O2 in sponge.

In general, CTFs can be easily synthesized through cyclotrimerization reaction of nitrile moieties via trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TfOH) catalysis [25–27]. Nonetheless, they always display a very low H2O2 production rate without incorporation of electron-withdrawing and/or electron-donating functionalities due to the low charge separation efficiency and slow charge transfer kinetics. To boost charge separation and transport for H2O2 production in a water and oxygen mixture, we hence introduced CDs and thiourea moieties onto CTFs through sequential trimerization reaction of 1,3-bis(4-cyanophenyl) thiourea (Bpt) and 1,4-dicyanobenzene (DC) mixed with CDs as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The resultant product named as CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs. To compare, the control samples of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs and Bpt@DC-CTFs were fabricated using DC, Bpt and both of them as reactive monomers under the same prepared conditions, respectively.

By Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy characterizations, all CTFs displayed two strong peaks at 1509 and 1340 cm−1 (Fig. 1b) assigning to the stretching and breathing modes of aromatic C-N, suggesting the formation of triazine rings. The peak at 1580 cm−1 is associated with the C=C stretching of phenyl groups. Distinguished from DC-CTFs, Bpt-based CTFs including Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs presented a new vibrational peak at 1165 cm−1, which can be attributed to −C=S derived from Bpt precursor [28,29]. To confirm these results, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the chemical structures of CTFs. According to XPS C 1s spectra (Fig. 1c-f), three chemical bonds of C–C, C=N and C–N were observed from all CTFs. Besides, the C=S bonds also appeared in the XPS C 1s spectra of Bpt-based CTFs [28]. Most importantly, a new fitting peak related with O=C–OH was found in the XPS C 1s spectra of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs, indicating the coexistence of CDs. Meanwhile, the morphology of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs was observed using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Fig. 1 g and h). The CTFs were stacked each other and formed the large flakes without clear lattice fringes, in agreement with XRD patterns (Figure S2). The typical high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image presented a carbon nanoparticle with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1i), which matches with (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon. The corresponding elemental mapping of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs showed the coexistence and uniform distribution of C, N, O and S elements (Fig. 1j), implying the uniform incorporation of CDs into CTFs as well.

Fig. 2 a shows the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of all CTFs. DC-CTFs mainly absorbed short wavelength light (<400 nm) while Bpt-based CTFs exhibited superior absorption performance in ultraviolet and visible region. It is worth noting that the incorporation of CD into CTFs extended visible light response. The Tauc plots showed that the bandgaps of DC-CTFs, Bpt-CTFs, Bpt@DC-CTFs and CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs are 3.08, 2.24, 2.55, 2.31 eV (Figure S3), respectively. Then, the band edge potentials of the samples (Fig. 2b) were determined in combination with the valence band maximum (VBM) measured by XPS valence band (VB) spectra (Figure S4). It can be found that the energy level of the VBM of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs is located below the oxidation level for H2O2/O2 (0.68 eV vs. RHE) but above than that for H2O2/H2O (1.78 eV vs. RHE). Accordingly, CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs with appropriate band energy position can realize the ORR and WOR simultaneously [30].

Fig. 2c provides the H2O2 production over as-prepared samples without adding any sacrificial agents at room temperature under solar irradiation. CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs exhibited the highest H2O2 production rate (373 µmol·g−1·h−1) among the samples, which is about 31.1, 4.8, and 1.9 times higher than DC-CTFs (12 µmol·g−1·h−1), Bpt-CTFs (79 µmol·g−1·h−1) and Bpt@DC-CTFs (194 µmol·g−1·h−1), respectively. We also explored the influence of the added amount of CDs and mass ratio of DC and Bpt on the H2O2 production rate of the CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs. From Fig. 2d and e, the optimal amount of CDs added in the preparing system is 3.0 mg (mass concentration 1.2 %), and the best mass ratio of DC and Bpt is 1:1.5, implying that the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared composite catalyst is determined by components. Fig. 2f shows the photocurrent responses of all CTFs under one sun irradiation to understand photoexcited electron − hole separation behaviors. By comparison, the electron − hole separation efficiency of DC-CTFs was lowest, while that of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs was highest among four samples. Accordingly, CDs facilitated photoinduced charge separation and migration, resulting in the high current output. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) verified that the electron transfer resistance across the electrode/electrolyte interface of the CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs was variously smaller than that Bpt@DC-CTFs as well as DC-CTFs and Bpt-CTFs (Fig. 2g). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra can reveal the irradiative recombination of charge carriers. Compared to Bpt@DC-CTFs, PL emission intensity for CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs is lower (Fig. 2h), reflecting the enhanced charge separation and transfer efficiency [31]. By the transient fluorescence spectroscopy (TFS) characterizations (Fig. 2i), the average fluorescence lifetime (Table S1) of the photoexcited electrons form CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs (4.35 ns) is longer than that of Bpt@DC-CTFs (1.41 ns), proving that reasonable incorporation of CDs into CTFs is conducive to efficient separation of carriers through prolonging their lifetime [32,33]. More interestingly, the increment of H2O2 production rate of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs was dramatically smaller than that of Bpt@DC-CTFs after adding hole sacrifice agent of ethanol in photocatalytic reaction system (Figure S5). Meanwhile, the decline of H2O2 production rate of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs was slower than that of Bpt@DC-CTFs when photocatalytic reaction occurs under N2-saturated atmosphere using AgNO3 as electron sacrificial agent (Figure S6). These phenomena indicated that CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs enabled more photogenerated holes to conduct WOR via efficient charge separation and transfer.

Note that the above as-prepared photocatalysts still suffer from the relatively low H2O2 production rate though the activity of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs is enhanced significantly with the help of CDs. To further elevate H2O2 yield, we adopted a new strategy without undergoing complicated material design and synthesis. First of all, the photocatalysts of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs were dispersed into the aqueous solution of PVA and glutaraldehyde (GA). Then, a porous melamine sponge was immersed into the above obtained solution to make CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs graft on its skeleton surface by in-situ polymerization and cross-linking of PVA molecules triggered with HCl [34] as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. Of note, PVA gel networks not only stabilized CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs on the spongy skeleton, but also reduced evaporation enthalpyof water through hydrophilic groups to boost vapor generation [35,36]. Finally, the photocatalyst-modified sponge after water adsorption saturation was then encapsulated in a transparent glass box (labeled as Device 1) to perform photocatalytic reaction under one sun irradiation. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of photocatalyst-modified sponge show that the skeleton of porous sponge was wrapped by CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs and PVA gel, but open porous systems of the sponge remained (Fig. 3b-d). As thus, the Device 1 exhibited a strong light absorption across the solar spectrum (Figure S7).

Upon one sun irradiation, we compared H2O2 yields in device and conventional particulate photocatalysis (PC) under the same amounts of water and catalyst. From Fig. 3e, the H2O2 concentration of Device 1 reached 105.2 mM·g−1·h−1, much higher than that of PC (56.1 mM·g−1·h−1). Moreover, two control experiments were carried out for photocatalysis. One was that the photocatalyst-modified sponge located in underwater (Control 1, Figure S9), the other was solar interfacial catalysis (Control 2, Figure S10), which the photocatalyst-modified sponge put into a home-made cylinder container of polystyrene foam connected cotton threads as water pumping channel. As a result, the H2O2 concentration produced from Control 1 was 22.9 mM·g−1·h−1 while that of Control 2 attained 63.6 mM·g−1·h−1. To shed light on the reason, we monitored the surface temperature changes on the photocatalyst-modified sponge for different cases. As shown in Fig. 3f and Figure S11, the temperature increment of Device 1 exceeded 20 ℃, which is more than twice that of Control 1 and Control 2. The high temperature upon Device 1 should be attributed to the suppression for tremendous energy consumption of evaporation compared to Control 2. The solar-converted heat in Control 1 was easily dissipated into the bulk water, thus giving rise to the smallest temperature increment.

Solar-converted heat was mainly stored in vapor. The flowing vapor carried O2 shuttled through the channels in sponge and then allowed more chances to touch with active sites of photocatalysts. Meanwhile, the high temperature induced by vapor-released latent heat not only could energize the active sites of photocatalysts, but also could make the reaction activity of H2O and O2 improve, facilitating both thermodynamics and reaction kinetics for WOR and ORR. To understand the dependence of solar-converted heat on H2O2 yield, the system components of Device 1 were changed for tuning the solar-to-heat conversion efficiency. We combined Bpt@DC-CTFs with porous sponge to obtain Device 2 by the same processes with those of Device 1. Different with Device 1, the photocatalysts of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs were directly grafted on the skeleton of porous sponge with no addition of PVA gel for Device 3. It was clear that the Device 1 presented optimal in terms of not only photocatalytic performance but also the acquired heat (Figure S12). Both CDs and PVA gel had an important influence on solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency, leading to the difference in water evaporation performances (Figure S13).

Efficient solar-to-heat utilization promoted H2O2 yield in device. However, the dependence of H2O2 yield on the supplied heat is still needed to be determined by controlling input light intensities. We examined H2O2 yields from Device 1 under the different solar intensities (Fig. 4 a). The growth rate of H2O2 yield was first fast and then slows with the solar intensity increase from 0.5 to 2 sun. This phenomenon corresponded to the tested surface temperature changes with the solar intensities (Fig. 4b). Of note, the enlarged temperature increment under the solar intensity of 1.5 sun could result from the latent heat released by more vapor as generated during solar irradiation. But further increase of solar intensity would influence the cooling of vapor and limited the latent heat release of vapor. The proper thermal energy as provided in photocatalytic process boosted the H2O2 generation, but the exorbitant heat could incur the decomposition of H2O2 (Fig. 4c). Hence, there was an optimal value for solar intensity as used in photosynthesis. Furthermore, the limited oxygen content in water should be alsotothedisadvantageof ORR, which is a critically important half-reaction of H2O2 formation for CTFs-based photocatalysts[37,38] (Figure S14). According to the variation of H2O2 yield with irradiation time (Figure S15), the progressive increase of H2O2 yield was interrupted after continuous 3 h irradiation because H2O2 production amount fell behind the decomposition of H2O2. Nevertheless, the enhancement of H2O2 yield regained after injecting oxygen in Device 1.

Most importantly, the H2O2 generation was reserved in the sponge, realizing a concentrated effect. For application of H2O2 as an oxidizing agent to degrade organic pollutant removal, the critical concentration is often needed to trigger oxidation reaction of organic compounds [39]. We here investigated the degradation of three typical organic pollutants including Rhodamine B (RhB), phenol and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), which is a type of antibiotic globally produced and used (Note: residual TC in water causes environmental pollution and the emergence of antibiotic-resistant pathogens, threatening human health[40,41]), using Device and PC under solar irradiation. Under the same photocatalytic conditions, Device 1 demonstrates much superior photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB: a 98 % degradation ratio was reached after 1 h, but with PC, only 22 % degradation was achieved (Fig. 4d). Similar trend for photocatalytic degradation of TC and phenol was observed with that of RhB. Device 1 also exhibits remarkably higher photocatalytic activity in removing TC and phenol than PC (Fig. 4e and 4f). The organic degradation rate for Device 1 surpassed four times higher than that for PC. All of these results verified that the high concentration of H2O2 produced by Device was more applicable to rapid degradation of organic pollutants.

In conclusion, CTFs-based metal-free photocatalysts were rationally designed and prepared for efficient H2O2 production from water and O2. We found that the incorporation of thiourea groups and CDs can facilitate the photogenerated carrier separation and transfer on CTFs and definitely enhance H2O2 yield. However, the H2O2 concentration produced from conventional PC remained low relative to the practice application in the pollutant removal. When as-prepared photocatalysts of CDs + Bpt@DC-CTFs were grafted on the skeleton of porous sponge and then encapsulated in a transparent glass box, the resultant Device 1 achieved 105.2 mM of H2O2 for per gram photocatalyst in 1 h, close to twice PC. This is attributed to both the efficient utilization of solar-converted heat and sponge storage function for H2O2. Moreover, we demonstrated that our developed Device 1 exhibited much more superior activity to degrade different organic pollutants such as RhB, phenol and TC compared with conventional PC owing to high-concentration H2O2 acquired by the same photocatalytic conditions. Therefore, this work may pave a new avenue for designing artificial photosynthesis system to achieve high-concentration H2O2 from solar energy.

Wenjia Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jizong Song: Formal analysis, Data curation. Weijie Ren: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jie Wu: Funding acquisition, Resources. Qing Chang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources. Ning Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Jinlong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Shengliang Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly supported by the special fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province (202304051001014), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037) and Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151796.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Small
